scores.system_answer:
## 1) How the 2025 Direction defines (and expands) the concept of a “Customer”

### (a) “Customer” is defined by *engagement in any financial transaction or activity*—not merely by account-holding
- The 2025 Direction defines **“Customer”** as **“a person who is engaged in a financial transaction or activity with the bank”** and **“includes a person on whose behalf the person who is engaged in the transaction or activity, is acting.”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (iii))]

### (b) The framework explicitly brings “walk-in” users of banking services into the customer perimeter
- It defines **“Walk-in Customer”** as a person **“who does not have an account-based relationship with the bank, but undertakes transactions with the bank.”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (iv))]

### (c) Practical breadth: “transaction” includes many banking activities (beyond deposits/withdrawals)
- **“Transaction”** is defined broadly and **“includes”**: **“opening of an account”**, **“deposit, withdrawal, exchange or transfer of funds… by electronic or other non-physical means”**, **“the use of a safety deposit box”**, **“entering into any fiduciary relationship”**, and more. [C. Definitions, p.10 (Paragraph (xx))]

> What is *not provided in the retrieved sections*: Any comparative text showing how the 2025 Direction changed the definition versus an earlier Direction. The conclusion that it “redefines” is therefore addressed strictly by explaining the 2025 definition and its operational breadth (account-based + non-account-based activities), without making claims about prior definitions. [C. Definitions, pp.10-11]

---

## 2) How this breadth impacts implementation of Customer Due Diligence (CDD) across banking activities

### (a) CDD is framed as identification + verification of both customer and beneficial owner
- **“Customer Due Diligence (CDD)”** is defined as **“identifying and verifying the customer and the beneficial owner using reliable and independent sources of identification.”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (v))]
- The **Explanation** to CDD states that, at relevant trigger points, CDD **“shall include”**:
  - **“Identification of the customer, verification of their identity… obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship, where applicable”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (v), Explanation (a))]
  - **“Taking reasonable steps to understand the nature of the customer's business, and its ownership and control”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (v), Explanation (b))]
  - **“Determining whether a customer is acting on behalf of a beneficial owner… [and] verify the identity of the beneficial owner”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (v), Explanation (c))]

### (b) Because “customer” includes walk-ins and non-account interactions, CDD/CIP must extend to specified non-account activities and thresholds
The Direction operationalises the broad “customer” concept through **Customer Identification Procedure (CIP)** triggers in **Paragraph 21**:
- **Account opening / onboarding**: Identification at **“Commencement of an account-based relationship”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(1))]
- **International money transfer for non-account holders**: Identification for **“any international money transfer operations for a person who is not an account holder of the bank.”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(2))]
- **Threshold-based “occasional” / non-account activity**:
  - Identification when **selling**: **“third-party products as agents, selling its own products, payment of dues of credit cards / sale and reloading of prepaid / travel cards and any other product for more than ₹50,000.”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(4))]
  - Identification for **walk-in customers**: **“transactions for a non-account-based customer, i.e., a walk-in customer, where the amount involved is equal to or exceeds ₹50,000, whether conducted as a single transaction or several transactions that appear to be connected.”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(5))]
  - Anti-structuring trigger: Identification when the bank believes a customer is **“intentionally structuring a transaction into a series of transactions below the threshold of ₹50,000.”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(6))]
- **Data-quality trigger**: Identification **“when there is a doubt about the authenticity or adequacy of the customer identification data.”** [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(3))]

Additionally, the CDD definition’s Explanation independently fixes the core trigger points as:
- **“at the time of commencement of an account-based relationship”** OR
- **“while carrying out occasional transaction of an amount equal to or exceeding ₹50,000… or any international money transfer operations”** [C. Definitions, p.11 (Paragraph (v), Explanation)]

### (c) The broadened “customer” perimeter changes *who* must be subjected to CDD within entity relationships (not only the named account-holder)
The Direction requires CDD collection for individuals not only as direct customers but also when they act within a legal-entity relationship:
- For CDD, a bank shall obtain specified information/documents from an individual **“while establishing an account-based relationship or while dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner, authorised signatory or the power of attorney holder related to any legal entity.”** [Chapter VI – Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure – A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25 (Paragraph 23 chapeau)]

### (d) The breadth of “customer” also drives differentiated CDD/EDD controls by delivery channel and risk classification
- The bank must adopt a risk-based approach, including categorising customers into **“low, medium, and high-risk categories”** and may consider parameters including **“delivery channel used for delivery of products / services”** and **“types of transactions undertaken…”** [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22 (Paragraph 20(1) and 20(3))]
- For **non-face-to-face customer onboarding** (other than onboarding under Paragraph 25), the Direction mandates **Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)** measures, and explicitly defines the category: such onboarding establishes a relationship **“without meeting the customer physically or through V-CIP.”** [F. Enhanced and Simplified Due Diligence Procedure – F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47 (Paragraph 44 chapeau)]
  - If the bank has V-CIP, it **“shall provide the same as the first option to the customer for remote onboarding”**, and **“shall treat processes… for V-CIP on par with face-to-face CIP for the purpose of these Directions.”** [F. Enhanced and Simplified Due Diligence Procedure – F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47 (Paragraph 44(1))]
  - EDD controls include: positive confirmation of current address **“before allowing operations”**, PAN collection and verification, first credit from existing KYC-complied account, and mandatory high-risk categorisation and enhanced monitoring until face-to-face/V-CIP verification. [F. Enhanced and Simplified Due Diligence Procedure – F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47 (Paragraph 44(3) to 44(6))]

### (e) Breadth of customer perimeter requires CDD to be sustained through ongoing due diligence and periodic KYC updation (not just at onboarding)
- The bank **“shall undertake ongoing due diligence of customers”** to ensure transactions are consistent with the bank’s knowledge of the customer, the customer’s business/risk profile, and source of funds/wealth. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44 (Paragraph 39)]
- The bank must align monitoring to risk category and review risk categorisation **“at least once in every six months”** and establish the need for enhanced due diligence. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44 (Paragraph 41(1))]
- The Direction prescribes minimum periodic KYC updation frequencies: **“at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every 10 years for low-risk customers.”** [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44 (Paragraph 42(1))]

---

## 3) Consolidated compliance implication across banking activities (what to operationalise)

Given the Direction’s “customer” definition (transaction/activity-based + on-behalf-of + walk-ins), banks must implement CDD/CIP controls in at least these activity clusters:
- **Account-based relationships** (all customer types): identification at commencement, followed by ongoing due diligence and periodic KYC updation. [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(1)); E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44 (Paragraphs 39 and 42(1))]
- **Non-account-based and occasional transactions**, specifically when:
  - Walk-in transactions are **≥ ₹50,000** (single or connected), or suspected structuring below ₹50,000, or specified product sales exceed ₹50,000. [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(4) to 21(6))]
  - International money transfer operations are undertaken for a non-account holder. [Chapter V – Customer Identification Procedure (CIP), pp.23-24 (Paragraph 21(2))]
- **Legal-entity banking** where CDD must encompass the persons behind/acting for the entity (beneficial owners, authorised signatories, power of attorney holders) using the individual CDD standards in Paragraph 23. [Chapter VI – Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure – A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25 (Paragraph 23 chapeau); C. CDD Measures for Legal Entities, pp.37-38 (Paragraphs 33-36)]
- **Channel-driven onboarding**: if the relationship is established through **non-face-to-face onboarding** (as defined in Paragraph 44), EDD measures apply and customers must be high-risk until face-to-face or V-CIP verification. [F. Enhanced and Simplified Due Diligence Procedure – F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47 (Paragraph 44(6))]
scores.reference_answer: The definition of a 'Customer' under Paragraph 5(2)(iii) is remarkably broad, encompassing any person engaged in a financial transaction or activity with the bank, including those on whose behalf the transaction is being conducted. This conceptualization moves beyond simple account-holders to include 'Walk-in Customers' (Paragraph 5(2)(iv)) who may only interact with the bank for occasional transactions exceeding ₹50,000. By including persons acting on behalf of others, the Direction forces banks to look through the immediate transactor to identify the ultimate initiator. This necessitates a multi-layered CDD process that is not just transactional but also relational, requiring the bank to understand the 'intended nature of the business relationship' even for non-account-based interactions. The impact is a systemic shift where every touchpoint—whether it be a wire transfer, a foreign exchange transaction, or a simple deposit—becomes a potential KYC event if it crosses specific thresholds or suggests a pattern of 'structuring'.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Role of the Risk Based Approach (RBA) in the 2025 Directions (regulatory intent → core operating principle)

### A. RBA is a mandatory, bank-wide risk mitigation framework anchored in governance
- **Paragraph 11** requires that **“The bank shall apply a Risk Based Approach (RBA) for mitigation and management of the risks (identified on its own or through national risk assessment) and shall have Board-approved policies, controls and procedures in this regard.”** This establishes RBA as the organising principle for AML/CFT risk mitigation, not a discretionary overlay. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- The Directions also embed RBA within the **Board-approved KYC policy architecture** by requiring the KYC policy to include **“Risk Management”** and **“Monitoring of Transactions”** as two of its **“four key elements”**. [Chapter II – General – A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17] (Paragraph 6(2)(ii), Paragraph 6(2)(iv))

### B. RBA is operationalised through customer risk categorisation and risk-sensitive controls
- Under **Chapter IV – Risk Management**, the bank’s risk-based approach is expressly defined to include **categorising customers into “low, medium, and high-risk categories”** based on the bank’s own assessment and risk perception. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(1))
- The Directions provide a non-exhaustive but detailed set of **risk parameters** for risk categorisation—e.g., **“customer’s identity, social / financial status, nature of business activity… geographical risk… type of products / services… delivery channel… types of transactions undertaken…”** and allow factoring **“the ability to confirm identity documents through online or other services offered by issuing authorities”**. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(3))
- Risk categorisation is treated as sensitive: the bank must keep both **“the risk categorisation of a customer and the specific reasons for such categorisation confidential”** to avoid tipping off, while still collecting **“various other non-intrusive information… relating to the perceived risk”** and specifying it in the KYC policy. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(4))

### C. RBA shapes due diligence intensity across the customer lifecycle (onboarding → monitoring → periodic KYC)
- **CDD programme design** is explicitly tied to RBA: **Paragraph 11** mandates implementation of **“a CDD programme, having regard to the ML / TF risks identified and the size of business.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- **Ongoing due diligence and monitoring** are then calibrated to risk:
  - Transactions must be monitored for consistency with the bank’s knowledge of the customer and their risk profile. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 39)
  - The bank must **“align the extent of monitoring with the risk category of the customer.”** [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41)
  - Risk categorisation must be periodically reviewed **“at least once in every six months”** to establish whether enhanced due diligence is needed. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41(1))
- **Periodic updation of KYC** is also expressly risk-based, with minimum periodicities hard-coded by customer risk rating: **“at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every 10 years for low-risk customers.”** [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))

## 2) How Paragraph 11 bridges the gap between regulatory intent and operational execution (especially across banks of varying size and complexity)

Paragraph 11 functions as the “translation layer” from principle-level regulatory expectations (RBA) to implementable bank controls in three ways:

### A. It converts “RBA” from a concept into concrete institutional obligations
Paragraph 11 does not stop at requiring an RBA; it requires:
- **Governance artefacts**: **“Board-approved policies, controls and procedures”** (i.e., documented and accountable control design). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- **Implementation**: the bank must **“implement a CDD programme”** (i.e., operational execution, not only policy statements). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- **Control assurance loop**: the bank must **“monitor the implementation of the controls and enhance them if necessary.”** This creates an ongoing supervisory-quality feedback cycle (design → deploy → monitor → improve). [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)

### B. It expressly builds proportionality for banks of different sizes by linking CDD programme design to “size of business”
- The key proportionality mechanism is explicit: the bank must implement the CDD programme **“having regard to the ML / TF risks identified and the size of business.”** [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- This aligns with the Directions’ requirement that the bank’s internal ML/TF risk assessment be **“proportionate to the nature, size, geographical presence, complexity of activities / structure, etc. of the bank.”** [Chapter II – General – A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17] (Paragraph 10(2))

Operationally, the combined effect is that:
- Banks are not directed to run a “one-size-fits-all” CDD programme; rather, both the **risk assessment** and the **CDD programme** are meant to scale with (a) the risks the bank faces and (b) the bank’s own size and complexity. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11); [Chapter II – General – A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17] (Paragraph 10(2))

### C. It connects high-level RBA intent to specific operating modules already defined elsewhere in the Directions
Paragraph 11 is implementable because the Directions supply downstream operational components that naturally “plug into” an RBA framework, including:
- **Risk categorisation mechanics** (low/medium/high; parameter-based assessment; confidentiality) that can be embedded into policies and procedures. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(1), 20(3), 20(4))
- **Risk-calibrated monitoring** (monitoring aligned to customer risk category; periodic review at least every six months). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41, Paragraph 41(1))
- **Risk-based periodic KYC updation** with minimum frequencies by risk category, and a requirement to document the bank’s policy as part of its internal KYC policy approved by the Board (or empowered Board committee). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))
- **Risk escalation into EDD**, particularly for specified onboarding contexts (e.g., non-face-to-face onboarding other than paragraph 25 is subject to specified EDD measures and must be categorised as high-risk with enhanced monitoring until face-to-face verification or V-CIP). [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47] (Paragraph 44(6))

## What is not fully available in the retrieved sections
- Your question references the **“2025 Directions”** and asks specifically how Paragraph 11 bridges intent and execution across varying sizes/complexities. The retrieved excerpts provide Paragraph 11 and multiple connected provisions; however, **the retrieved set does not include any additional RBI commentary/annexures (if any) explaining Paragraph 11’s purpose, examples of proportional implementation, or supervisory expectations on how smaller vs larger banks should evidence proportionality beyond the explicit phrases cited**. Therefore, this answer confines itself to the explicit text and logical linkage across the retrieved provisions. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11); [Chapter II – General – A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17] (Paragraph 10(2))
scores.reference_answer: The Risk Based Approach (RBA) is the cornerstone of the modern AML/CFT framework, as articulated in Paragraph 11. It requires banks to apply mitigation measures that are strictly proportionate to the risks identified through their internal 'Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Risk Assessment' exercises. Conceptually, Paragraph 11 bridges the gap between intent and execution by mandating that the CDD program must have 'regard to the ML/TF risks identified and the size of business'. For a large, internationally active bank, this implies highly sophisticated, automated monitoring and a granular categorization of risks across different geographies and products. For a smaller bank, it allows for a more focused but still documented risk assessment. This conceptual flexibility prevents a 'one-size-fits-all' compliance burden while ensuring that higher-risk areas—such as cross-border correspondent banking—are subjected to 'Enhanced Due Diligence' (EDD). The execution is further reinforced by Paragraph 12, which requires the bank to specify who constitutes 'Senior Management' for the purpose of KYC compliance, thereby linking the RBA directly to internal corporate governance.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Conceptual significance of identifying the Beneficial Owner (BO) in the 2025 framework

Within the 2025 KYC framework, the concept of the “Beneficial Owner (BO)” is used to **collapse a legal person’s (company / firm / association / trust) formal legal structure into the accountable “natural person(s)”** who ultimately own and/or control it.

Key elements showing this conceptual role:

- **BO is defined as a “natural person(s)” even where ownership/control is exercised indirectly**, i.e., “whether acting alone or together, or through one or more juridical persons” (for companies / partnership firms / unincorporated associations). This explicitly targets layered or interposed entities by requiring a look-through to the natural person(s). [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)-(c)]
- The BO definition is not limited to equity/profit entitlement; it also captures **“control through other means”**, meaning that the framework treats **control-based influence as equally important as formal ownership** for identifying the true responsible person(s). [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)-(b)]
- For trusts, the framework conceptualises BO identification as covering **the key fiduciary actors and the “ultimate effective control” chain**, requiring identification of “the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.” [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(d)]

This definition is then operationalised as an onboarding obligation:

- “For opening an account of a Legal Person who is not a natural person, the bank shall identify the beneficial owner(s) and shall undertake all reasonable steps… to verify their identity….” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39, Paragraph 38]

## 2) The hierarchy (ownership → control → senior managing official) and why it matters

### A. Ownership test (primary route)
The BO framework first anchors identification to **quantitative ownership/entitlement thresholds** (i.e., objective, measurable indicators):

- **Company:** BO includes natural person(s) with “a controlling ownership interest,” where “controlling ownership interest” is defined as “ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of the shares or capital or profits of the company.” [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)]
- **Partnership firm:** BO includes natural person(s) with “ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership.” [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(b)]
- **Unincorporated association / body of individuals:** BO includes natural person(s) with “ownership of / entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits….” [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(c)]
- **Trust:** BO identification includes “the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust….” [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(d)]

### B. Control test (secondary route)
Where ownership alone may not reveal the real decision-maker, the framework requires identifying natural person(s) who **exercise “control”**:

- **Company:** BO includes the natural person(s) “who exercises control through other means,” and “Control” is expansively defined to include “the right to appoint the majority of the directors or to control the management or policy decisions including by virtue of their shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements.” [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)]
- **Partnership firm:** BO includes those who “exercise control through other means,” where “control” includes “the right to control the management or policy decision.” [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(b)]

### C. Senior managing official fallback (anti-avoidance backstop)
To prevent BO identification from failing merely because ownership/control is structured to be diffuse or opaque, the framework provides a definitive backstop:

- “Where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person who holds the position of senior managing official.” [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv) (Explanation following sub-clause (c))]

## 3) How this hierarchy protects the financial system from opaque legal arrangements

The protection comes from three linked design features visible in the text:

### 3.1 Look-through across layers and aggregation of persons
The definition repeatedly requires identification of natural persons even where they operate indirectly or collectively—capturing structures designed to obscure individuals behind entities or fragmentation.

- BO is the natural person(s) even if acting “together” or “through one or more juridical persons” (company / partnership / association). This directly addresses opacity from multi-layer entity chains. [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)-(c)]
- For trusts, BO identification expressly extends to any person exercising “ultimate effective control… through a chain of control or ownership,” which targets complex trust layering and control chains. [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(d)]

### 3.2 Dual lens: economic ownership + governance/control rights
Opaque structures often separate economics (benefit) from power (control). The 2025 framework explicitly requires both:

- Ownership thresholds (“more than 10 percent…”, “more than 15 percent…”, “10 percent or more interest…”) identify those with meaningful economic stake. [C. Definitions, pp.5-6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)-(d)]
- Control definition for companies includes governance levers (appoint directors; control management/policy; shareholder/voting agreements), capturing control that may be hidden from simple cap-table checks. [C. Definitions, p.5, Paragraph 5(1)(iv)(a)]

### 3.3 No-dead-end identification: mandatory fallback to a natural person
If neither ownership nor control yields a natural person (e.g., deliberately dispersed shareholding or layered control), the framework forces identification of a responsible natural person via the senior managing official fallback.

- The senior managing official fallback (“Where no natural person is identified…”) acts as a structural safeguard against legal arrangements that would otherwise leave the bank without a human BO to verify. [C. Definitions, p.6, Paragraph 5(1)(iv) (Explanation)]

## 4) Operational reinforcement in onboarding and lifecycle KYC

The BO concept is not merely definitional; it is reinforced through account-opening and ongoing processes:

- **Account-opening obligation for legal persons:** the bank “shall identify the beneficial owner(s)” and “shall undertake all reasonable steps… to verify their identity….” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39, Paragraph 38]
- **CDD documentation capture for BO:** for companies / partnership firms / trusts / unincorporated associations, the bank must obtain “Documents, as specified in paragraph 23, relating to beneficial owner…” as part of account opening documentation. [C. CDD Measures for Legal Entities, pp.37-38, Paragraph 33(5); Paragraph 34(4); Paragraph 35(4); Paragraph 36(4)]
- **Ongoing accuracy of BO information for non-individuals:** during periodic updation where there is “No change in KYC information” for the legal entity (LE) customer, the bank “shall ensure during this process that Beneficial Ownership (BO) information available with them is accurate and shall update the same, if required, to keep it as up-to-date as possible.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44, Paragraph 42(4)(i)]

## 5) What the provided extracts do NOT specify

- The provided sections define BO and impose identification/verification and updating obligations, but **do not explicitly articulate a broader policy statement** (e.g., a formal objective clause) describing the “conceptual significance” in terms of financial-system integrity. The conceptual explanation above is therefore grounded strictly in the definitional structure and obligations shown in the text. [C. Definitions, pp.5-6; D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]
scores.reference_answer: The identification of the Beneficial Owner (BO) is conceptually designed to peel back the layers of corporate and legal structures to find the natural person who ultimately owns or controls the entity. Under Paragraph 5(1)(iv), the framework establishes a clear hierarchy of identification. First, it looks for 'controlling ownership interest'—defined as ownership of more than 10 percent of shares or capital in companies and partnerships. Second, if ownership is fragmented, it looks for 'control' through management rights or voting agreements. Finally, if no natural person is identifiable through these means, the 'senior managing official' is deemed the BO. This conceptual progression is critical because it ensures there is always a natural person accountable for the entity's actions. It specifically protects against the use of 'shell' structures where legal ownership might be hidden behind a chain of juridical persons, thereby fulfilling the FATF's mandate for transparency in the financial system.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Digital KYC – alignment with face-to-face identification standards (conceptual position)

### Conceptual anchor: **physical co-presence with an authorised bank official**
- Under the **Digital KYC Process**, the regulation requires that **“[t]he customer, for the purpose of KYC, shall visit the location of the authorised official of the bank or vice-versa.”** (Paragraph **24(3)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27]. This embeds Digital KYC in a **physical, in-person interaction model** (customer and authorised official meet at the same location, one traveling to the other).
- Digital KYC further operationalises this in-person standard by requiring capture of **“a Live photograph of the customer”** embedded in the CAF, with a readable watermark containing **CAF number, GPS coordinates, authorised official’s name, unique employee code, date and time stamp** (Paragraph **24(4)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27].

### Conceptual implication for “face-to-face alignment”
- Because Digital KYC is performed **through a bank-controlled application** (Paragraph **24(1)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27] **and** mandates **physical meeting** (Paragraph **24(3)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27], its identification assurance is conceptually aligned to face-to-face standards through **direct physical availability of the customer and original OVD** (Paragraph **24(3)**: “The original OVD shall be in possession of the customer.”) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27].

## 2) V-CIP (Video-based Customer Identification Process) – alignment with face-to-face identification standards (conceptual position)

### Conceptual anchor: **real-time remote identification with “beyond doubt” quality and liveness controls**
- V-CIP is expressly structured as a video interaction process with strong assurance requirements, including that **“[t]he quality of the live video in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.”** (Paragraph **27(1)(iv)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31].
- The V-CIP infrastructure must include **“face liveness / spoof detection as well as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy”** while clarifying that **“ultimate responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank.”** (Paragraph **27(1)(v)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31].
- Operationally, V-CIP must be run by **“officials of the bank specially trained for this purpose”** who can conduct liveness checks and detect manipulation/suspicious conduct (Paragraph **27(2)(i)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32].

### How V-CIP achieves conceptual “equivalence” to face-to-face assurance (without requiring physical presence)
- In V-CIP, the authorised official must **“record audio and video as well as capture a photograph of the customer present for identification”** and obtain identification information using one of the specified sources: 
  - **OTP based Aadhaar e-KYC authentication**;
  - **Offline Verification of Aadhaar**;
  - **KYC records downloaded from CKYCR** (per Paragraph 65);
  - **Equivalent e-document of OVDs including documents issued through DigiLocker** (Paragraph **27(2)(vi)(a)–(d)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.33].
- V-CIP imposes additional control expectations typically associated with high-assurance identification, such as:
  - video recordings containing **“live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) … and date and time stamp”** (Paragraph **27(1)(iv)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31];
  - end-to-end encryption and auditable consent capture (Paragraph **27(1)(ii)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]; and
  - workflow controls that require rejection if **“any prompting at the customer end”** is observed (Paragraph **27(2)(iv)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.33].

### Regulatory positioning relative to non-face-to-face onboarding constraints
- The Directions explicitly describe **Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC** as being used **“in non-face-to-face mode”** (Paragraph **25**, heading) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29].
- Those non-face-to-face accounts cannot operate beyond one year unless identification is completed **“as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)”** (Paragraph **25(6)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]. This places V-CIP (Paragraphs 26–27) as a **full identification pathway** that can remediate limitations applicable to non-face-to-face Aadhaar OTP e-KYC onboarding.

## 3) Direct comparison — conceptual alignment with face-to-face standards

### Digital KYC
- **Conceptually aligned with face-to-face standards through physical meeting**: customer visits authorised official’s location or vice versa (Paragraph **24(3)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27].
- Uses digitisation primarily to strengthen evidencing (live photo embedding and watermarking with GPS/time and officer identifiers) (Paragraph **24(4)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.27].

### V-CIP
- **Conceptually aligned with face-to-face standards through real-time, controlled, high-assurance remote interaction**: “identification … beyond doubt,” liveness/spoof detection, trained bank officials, auditable recordings and geo-tagging (Paragraphs **27(1)(iv)**, **27(1)(v)**, **27(2)(i)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32].
- Unlike Digital KYC, V-CIP is not framed around physical co-presence; instead, it is framed around meeting identification assurance through video controls and prescribed identification information sources (Paragraph **27(2)(vi)**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.33].

## 4) What is not explicitly stated in the provided sections
- The provided excerpts do **not** explicitly state that V-CIP is “treated on par with face-to-face” or that it is categorised as “face-to-face” for all purposes. The text instead specifies minimum standards and where V-CIP can be used (Paragraphs **26–27**) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.30-33].
- The provided excerpts also do not include Paragraph 23’s content; therefore, the exact standard of “identification as per paragraph 23” cannot be quoted or directly compared here (Paragraph **25(6)** references it) [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30].
scores.reference_answer: Digital KYC and V-CIP are conceptually distinct yet complementary processes in the digital onboarding ecosystem. Digital KYC, as defined in Paragraph 5(1)(viii), is essentially an electronic version of a face-to-face interaction where an 'authorized officer' of the bank is physically present with the customer to capture live photos and documents along with geo-tagging (latitude and longitude). In contrast, V-CIP, defined in Paragraph 5(2)(xvi), is a remote process involving a seamless, informed-consent-based audio-visual interaction. While both utilize facial recognition, V-CIP is conceptually treated as 'on par with face-to-face CIP' despite the absence of physical proximity. This parity is achieved through stringent technical standards, including liveness checks, AI-driven face matching, and end-to-end encryption, which are designed to provide the same level of assurance—if not higher—than a traditional physical meeting.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Conceptual distinction in the 2025 Direction: “Ongoing Due Diligence” vs “Periodic Updation”

### A. Ongoing Due Diligence = continuous transaction-behaviour surveillance against an expected customer profile
- **Paragraph 39** frames ongoing due diligence as a *transactions-centric* control: the bank must “**undertake ongoing due diligence of customers to ensure that their transactions are consistent with their knowledge about the customers, customers’ business and risk profile, the source of funds / wealth**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 39)
- The Direction operationalises this as **monitoring for patterns and anomalies**, mandating that the bank “**shall necessarily monitor**” specified transaction-types, including: “**Large and complex transactions including RTGS transactions**… unusual patterns… no apparent economic rationale”, transactions exceeding prescribed thresholds, “**High account turnover**” inconsistent with balances, and “**Deposit of third-party cheques… followed by cash withdrawals** for large amounts.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 40(1)-(4))
- The **intensity must be risk-aligned**: the bank “**shall align the extent of monitoring with the risk category of the customer**,” and high-risk accounts are to be subjected to “**more intensified monitoring**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41 and Explanation)

### B. Periodic Updation = scheduled refresh of KYC/CDD records (information & documents), with minimum frequencies by risk category
- **Paragraph 42** conceptualises periodic updation as an *information/data governance* requirement: the bank must keep “**the information or data collected under CDD… up-to-date and relevant, particularly where there is high risk**,” using a risk-based approach. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))
- Unlike ongoing due diligence (which is event/transaction driven), periodic updation is **calendar-driven with minimum cycles**: “**at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every 10 years for low-risk customers**” (from account opening / last KYC updation). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))
- Periodic updation is also **process-specified** (e.g., self-declaration channels, address change confirmation timelines, BC enablement, PAN verification, acknowledgements, and notices/reminders). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(3)-(7)); [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47] (continuation of Paragraph 42(7))

**Net conceptual difference:**
- **Ongoing due diligence (Paragraphs 39–41)** is about **continuous monitoring of transactions** and behavioural consistency with the bank’s knowledge of the customer (including business, risk profile, and “source of funds / wealth”). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 39)
- **Periodic updation (Paragraph 42)** is about **periodic refreshing and validation of KYC/CDD information/documents** at specified minimum intervals, with defined procedural steps and customer communications. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1)-(7))


## 2) Systemic implications of Paragraph 39 (Ongoing Due Diligence)

### A. The bank must build/operate a monitoring system that compares real transactions to an “expected” customer profile
- Because Paragraph 39 requires consistency of transactions with the bank’s “knowledge about the customers… business and risk profile, the source of funds / wealth,” the bank’s systems must be capable of operationalising those reference points for monitoring. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 39)

### B. Mandatory scenario coverage for close monitoring (baseline typologies)
- Paragraph 40 makes certain monitoring **non-discretionary** (“shall necessarily monitor”) and therefore implies system rules/alerts should at least cover the enumerated categories, including RTGS-linked “large and complex transactions,” unusual patterns without apparent rationale, threshold breaches, high turnover, and cheque-deposit/cash-withdrawal patterns. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 40(1)-(4))

### C. Risk-tiered monitoring and periodic review of risk categorisation
- The bank must “align the extent of monitoring with the risk category” (i.e., risk-tiered surveillance). [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41)
- Paragraph 41(1) requires a **system of periodic review of risk categorisation**, with periodicity “**at least once in every six months**,” and an institutional mechanism to “**establish the need for applying enhanced due diligence measures**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41(1))
- The Direction elsewhere requires risk categorisation into “**low, medium, and high-risk categories**” as part of risk management. [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(1))

### D. Technology enablement is explicitly contemplated for ongoing due diligence
- For ongoing due diligence, the bank “**may consider adopting appropriate innovations including artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI and ML) technologies to support effective monitoring**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 40)
- Separately, to identify and report suspicious transactions effectively, the bank “**shall implement robust software that generates alerts when transactions are inconsistent with a customer's risk categorisation and updated profile**.” [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, pp.54-55] (Paragraph 55)

### E. Escalation / external reporting expectations embedded in ongoing monitoring
- For specified patterns in marketing firms / MLM accounts, the Explanation under Paragraph 41 indicates that cases exhibiting certain cheque-book and deposit/cheque issuance patterns “**shall be immediately reported to RBI and other appropriate authorities such as FIU-IND**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 41, Explanation)


## 3) Systemic implications of Paragraph 42 (Updation / Periodic Updation of KYC)

### A. Board-approved policy architecture and documentation is compulsory
- Paragraph 42(1) requires that the bank “**shall document its policy**” for periodic updation “**as part of the bank’s internal KYC policy duly approved by the Board of Directors… or any committee of the Board** to which power has been delegated.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))
- The broader KYC governance framework requires the bank to have a KYC policy approved by the Board (or delegated committee), and that KYC policy must include “**Periodic updation of KYC**” and “**Any exceptional measures for KYC updation**” (e.g., “requiring a recent photograph, physical presence, or a more frequent updation schedule than the minimum prescribed”). [A. Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines, pp.16-17] (Paragraph 6(1)-(3))

### B. Minimum periodicities by risk category drive enterprise-wide scheduling and controls
- Paragraph 42(1) sets minimum update cycles: **2 years (high risk), 8 years (medium risk), 10 years (low risk)** from account opening/last updation. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))
- This implies the bank’s KYC operations must be able to (i) track due dates and (ii) run periodic updation campaigns by risk category, consistent with its risk-based approach obligations. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(1))

### C. Overdue low-risk individual accounts: continued operations + monitoring + hard timeline
- Notwithstanding the normal periodicities, for a **low-risk individual**, the bank “**shall allow all transactions**” and ensure KYC updation “**within one year of its falling due for KYC or up to June 30, 2026, whichever is later**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(2))
- Even while allowing transactions, the bank “**shall subject accounts of such customers to regular monitoring**,” and this applies also where periodic updation “has already fallen due.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(2))

### D. Defined operating procedures for individuals and non-individuals (channel, verification, and equivalence to new onboarding)
- **Individuals (no change):** obtain “**a self-declaration**” through registered email/mobile/ATMs/digital channels/letter, etc. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(3)(i))
- **Individuals (change in address):** obtain self-declaration and “**verify the declared address through positive confirmation within two months**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(3)(ii))
- **Non-individuals (change in KYC information):** must “**undertake the KYC process equivalent to that applicable for onboarding a new LE customer**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(4)(ii))

### E. Controls at periodic updation: current CDD standards + PAN verification + customer acknowledgements
- Even where there is “no change,” the bank must ensure it has “**customer’s KYC document as per the current CDD standards**”; if documents are not as per current standards, or have expired, it must perform “**the KYC process equivalent to that applicable for onboarding a new customer**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(5)(i))
- The bank must “**verify the customer’s PAN details… from the database of the issuing authority**” at the time of periodic updation. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(5)(ii))
- The bank must provide an “**acknowledgment**” with date of receipt, “**promptly update**” records, and provide an “**intimation**” with date of updation. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(5)(iii))

### F. Customer communication, reminders, audit trail, and implementation deadline
- Prior to the due date, the bank must give “**at least three advance intimations, including at least one intimation by letter**” at appropriate intervals. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(7))
- The continuation of Paragraph 42(7) specifies: subsequent to due date, “**at least three reminders, including at least one reminder by letter**,” and the issuance “**shall be duly recorded… for audit trail**,” with implementation “**not later than January 01, 2026**.” [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47] (Paragraph 42(7), continuation)

### G. “Update within 30 days” trigger for customer-driven document changes
- The bank must advise customers that where documents submitted at onboarding/thereafter are updated, customers “**shall submit to the bank the update of such documents… within 30 days of the update**” for updating bank records. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraph 42(6))


## 4) How Paragraph 39 and Paragraph 42 operate systemically together (but remain conceptually distinct)

- The Direction requires the bank to implement a **Risk Based Approach (RBA)** and to “monitor the implementation of the controls and enhance them if necessary,” which is consistent with (i) ongoing transaction monitoring under Paragraph 39 and (ii) risk-based periodic updation cycles under Paragraph 42. [B. Other General Guidelines, pp.18-21] (Paragraph 11)
- Risk categorisation is a defined risk management construct (“low, medium, and high-risk categories”), which is then used as:
  - a determinant of **monitoring intensity** (Paragraph 41), and
  - a determinant of **minimum periodic updation frequency** (Paragraph 42(1)).
  [Chapter IV – Risk Management, p.22] (Paragraph 20(1)); [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraphs 41 and 42(1))


### What is not available in the provided sections
- The retrieved extracts do not provide any explicit *definitions section* that formally defines “ongoing due diligence” or “periodic updation” beyond the operative requirements in Paragraphs 39–42; therefore, the explanation above is grounded in those operative clauses rather than a separate definitions clause. [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44] (Paragraphs 39–42)
scores.reference_answer: 'Ongoing Due Diligence' (ODD) and 'Periodic Updation' represent two different dimensions of risk monitoring. ODD, under Paragraph 39, is a continuous, behavioral analysis of transactions to ensure they are consistent with the bank's knowledge of the customer's business and risk profile. It is reactive to transaction patterns. Periodic Updation, under Paragraph 42, is a time-bound, structural review of the customer's identity and address documents at specific intervals (e.g., every 2 years for high-risk, 10 years for low-risk). Systemically, ODD provides real-time protection against sudden shifts in behavior (such as money laundering through 'Money Mules'), while Periodic Updation ensures the bank's records remain legally and administratively current. Paragraph 39 specifically encourages the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI and ML) to support this continuous monitoring, marking a shift from manual oversight to automated systemic surveillance.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Concept of “Money Mules” under Paragraph 68
Paragraph 68 frames “Money Mules” as deposit accounts that are **“used to launder the proceeds of fraud schemes (e.g., phishing and identity theft)”** where criminals gain illegal access **“by recruiting third parties that act as ‘money mules’.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]

## 2) What Paragraph 68 requires banks to do (and where STR fits)
Paragraph 68 imposes a control-and-response obligation specifically aimed at preventing and detecting mule-account misuse:
- **Preventive / control obligation:** The bank must **“strictly adhere to the instructions on opening of accounts and monitoring of transactions”** to minimise money mule operations. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]
- **Detection obligation:** The bank must undertake **“diligence measures and meticulous monitoring to identify accounts which are operated as Money Mules”**. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]
- **Response obligation (includes STR reporting):** Once suspicious mule-type activity is identified, the bank must take **“appropriate action, including reporting of suspicious transactions to FIU-IND.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]

This fits within the broader FIU-IND reporting framework where:
- The bank **“shall furnish to the Director, Financial Intelligence Unit-India (FIUIND), the information referred to in Rule 3 of the PML (Maintenance of Records) Rules, 2005 in accordance with Rule 7 thereof.”** [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 52, pp.54-55]
- Banks are operationally enabled/expected to file **Suspicious Transaction Reports (STR)** through FIU-IND’s prescribed utilities/formats (including where suitable tools for extracting STR are not yet installed). [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 53, pp.54-55]
- Effective suspicion detection is reinforced by a systems requirement: **“To identify and report suspicious transactions effectively, the bank shall implement robust software that generates alerts when transactions are inconsistent with a customer's risk categorisation and updated profile.”** [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 55, pp.54-55]

## 3) Why failure to file an STR for a suspected/identified money mule is treated as a specific violation of these Directions
### (A) Paragraph 68 contains an explicit “deeming” non-compliance clause tied to STR non-filing
Paragraph 68 goes beyond a general expectation to report; it creates a specific compliance consequence:
- **“Further, if it is established that an account opened and operated is that of a Money Mule, but STR was not filed by the concerned bank, it shall then be deemed that the bank has not complied with these directions.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]

Within the framework of these Directions, this language makes STR filing a determinative compliance marker for mule accounts: if the account is later “established” as a money mule and no STR was filed, the bank is **deemed** non-compliant with the Directions themselves—independent of whether other controls were claimed.

### (B) STR non-filing contradicts the prescribed “appropriate action” for mule-type suspicion
Paragraph 68 expressly includes **“reporting of suspicious transactions to FIU-IND”** within “appropriate action” for money-mule identification/handling. Therefore, where mule operations are suspected/identified as suspicious, not reporting undermines the mandated response element of Paragraph 68. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]

### (C) The Directions also treat non-reporting as a violation event in the FIU-IND reporting chapter
The reporting chapter adds that:
- **“a delay of each day in not reporting a transaction… beyond the time limit as specified in the Rule shall constitute as a separate violation.”** [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55]

So, STR non-filing (or delayed filing beyond the applicable Rule timeframe) is not merely a process lapse; it is framed as a violation under the Directions’ reporting requirements as well.

## 4) What is not fully available in the provided extracts (limitations)
- Paragraph 68 describes the mule typology and prescribes diligence/monitoring and “appropriate action,” but the provided sections do **not** include a definition of “suspicious transaction” or the detailed STR triggers/time limits (these are referenced as being in the PML Rules). [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraphs 52 & 54, pp.54-55; Chapter X – Other Instructions, Paragraph 68, p.64]

scores.reference_answer: Money Mules are individuals recruited to launder proceeds of fraud (like phishing) by providing access to their deposit accounts. Paragraph 68 conceptualizes the bank's role as a proactive guardian. The failure to file a Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) for a mule account is treated as a deemed non-compliance with the Directions because it indicates a breakdown in the bank's monitoring systems. Conceptually, this places the onus on banks not just to identify criminals, but to identify the conduits they use—who may often be unwitting participants. This requires banks to move beyond simple identity verification to complex behavioral analysis of 'walk-in' and 'low-balance' accounts that suddenly exhibit high-turnover patterns.

## Foundational elements of a Customer Acceptance Policy (CAP) under Chapter III

### 1) CAP is a mandatory, formalised onboarding gatekeeper
- The bank **“shall frame a Customer Acceptance Policy”** (CAP). [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 16, pp.18-21]

### 2) Absolute prohibition on anonymous / fictitious / benami accounts
- CAP must ensure the bank **“not open any account in an anonymous or fictitious / benami name.”** This is the primary CAP control that directly blocks onboarding where the customer’s identity is not attributable to a real, identifiable person/entity. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(1), pp.18-21]

### 3) CDD is a pre-condition to onboarding and transactions (and failure to CDD is a hard stop)
CAP must ensure:
- The bank **“open no account where it is unable to apply appropriate CDD measures, either due to non-cooperation of the customer or unreliability of the documents / information furnished by the customer.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(2), pp.18-21]
  - Further, where the bank is unable to comply with relevant CDD measures, it **“shall consider filing an STR, if necessary.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(2), pp.18-21]
- The bank **“not undertake a transaction or commence an account-based relationship without following the CDD procedure.”** This operationalises the prohibition on anonymous entities by making CDD completion a mandatory onboarding and transaction prerequisite. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(3), pp.18-21]

### 4) Mandatory KYC information requirements (and consent for additional data)
CAP must:
- **“specify the mandatory information to be sought for KYC purposes while opening an account and during the periodic updation.”** This ensures a minimum, standardised identification dataset is collected so accounts are not created with missing identity attributes (a key enabler of anonymity). [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(4), pp.18-21]
- Where additional information is sought beyond the bank’s internal KYC policy specification, CAP must require the bank to **“obtain additional information… with the explicit consent of the customer.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(5), pp.18-21]

### 5) Consistent application of CDD at the UCIC level (prevents identity fragmentation)
- CAP must **“apply the CDD procedure at the UCIC level.”** Consequently, where an existing KYC-compliant customer seeks another account/product, **“there shall be no need for a fresh CDD exercise as far as identification of the customer is concerned.”** This reduces the risk of the same person being onboarded under multiple unlinked identities within the bank (a common pathway to effective anonymity). [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(6), pp.18-21]

### 6) Coverage for joint holders and representation / acting-on-behalf arrangements
CAP must ensure:
- **“follow the CDD Procedure for all the joint account holders”** when opening a joint account (so that unnamed/unchecked joint holders cannot be used to introduce anonymous ownership/control). [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(7), pp.18-21]
- **“clearly spell out the circumstances in which a customer is permitted to act on behalf of another person / entity.”** This is a foundational CAP control against anonymous principals operating behind agents/intermediaries without being identified. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(8), pp.18-21]

### 7) Sanctions / designated list screening as an acceptance filter
- CAP must require a system to ensure that the customer’s identity **“does not match with any person or entity, whose name appears in the sanctions lists indicated in Chapter IX of these Directions.”** This prevents onboarding of persons/entities that the bank must not maintain relationships with, thereby preventing acceptance of prohibited/opaque identities. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(9), pp.18-21]
  - Chapter IX operationalises this obligation by requiring the bank to ensure it **“does not have any account in the name of individuals / entities appearing in the lists… approved by and periodically circulated by the United Nations Security Council (UNSC)”** and to **“verify the… UNSC Sanctions Lists… on a daily basis.”** [Chapter IX – Requirements/obligations under International Agreements - Communications from International Agencies, Paragraph 56(1)-(2), pp.56-59]

### 8) Verification of key identifiers/documents to counter fictitious identities
CAP must require verifications that support authenticity and traceability:
- **PAN verification:** **“verify the Permanent Account Number (PAN) (if obtained) from the verification facility of the issuing authority.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(10), pp.18-21]
- **Digital signature verification:** **“verify the customer’s digital signature on the equivalent e-document (if obtained) as per the provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(11), pp.18-21]
- **GST verification:** **“verify the Goods and Services Tax (GST) number from the search / verification facility of the issuing authority, where the GST details are available.”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(12), pp.18-21]

### 9) Non-discrimination and documented decisioning (prevents blanket rejection while maintaining anti-anonymity controls)
- CAP **“shall not result in denial of a banking / financial facility to members of the general public, especially those who are financially or socially disadvantaged, including the Persons with Disabilities (PwDs).”** [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 18, pp.18-21]
- The bank **“shall not reject an application for onboarding or periodic updation of KYC without application of mind”** and **“shall duly record the reason(s) for rejection.”** This supports defensible, case-by-case rejection where anonymity/benami risk exists, rather than indiscriminate exclusion. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 18, pp.18-21]

### 10) Handling tip-off risk: when suspicion exists, pivot from CDD to STR
- Where the bank suspects ML/TF and reasonably believes CDD would tip-off the customer, it **“shall not pursue the CDD process, and instead file an STR with FIU-IND.”** This prevents customer interaction from enabling concealment and supports appropriate reporting. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 19, pp.18-21]

---

## How these CAP elements interact to prevent onboarding of anonymous entities (end-to-end logic)
- **First-line exclusion:** Paragraph 17(1) creates a categorical bar on anonymous/fictitious/benami names, setting the acceptance baseline. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(1), pp.18-21]
- **CDD as an operational “proof threshold”:** Paragraph 17(3) prohibits commencing relationships/transactions without CDD, and Paragraph 17(2) mandates refusal where CDD cannot be applied due to non-cooperation or unreliable information—closing off the primary routes by which anonymity could be preserved at onboarding. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(2)-(3), pp.18-21]
- **Minimum data completeness + authenticity checks:** Paragraph 17(4) ensures minimum KYC data is collected; Paragraph 17(10)-(12) require verification of key identifiers (PAN/digital signature/GST where applicable), reducing the risk that fabricated credentials create “anonymous in substance” customers. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(4), 17(10)-(12), pp.18-21]
- **No hiding behind structures:** Paragraph 17(7) ensures all joint holders undergo CDD; Paragraph 17(8) forces clear rules for acting-on-behalf, preventing unnamed principals from operating through representatives/intermediaries. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(7)-(8), pp.18-21]
- **External prohibition screen:** Paragraph 17(9), read with Chapter IX daily list verification, adds a sanctions filter so the bank does not onboard prohibited identities (including entities that may use opacity/aliases), reinforcing the “no anonymous/prohibited accounts” outcome. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 17(9), pp.18-21; Chapter IX – Requirements/obligations under International Agreements - Communications from International Agencies, Paragraph 56(1)-(2), pp.56-59]
- **If suspicion escalates, do not validate the anonymous actor via CDD:** Paragraph 19 requires STR filing instead of continuing CDD where tip-off risk exists, ensuring the bank does not inadvertently assist a potentially anonymous/illicit actor in adapting their behaviour to pass onboarding checks. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 19, pp.18-21]

---

## What is not available in the provided extracts
- The query asks for “foundational elements” of CAP **as per Chapter III**; the provided sections include Paragraphs 16–19 of Chapter III, but do not include any additional CAP content beyond Paragraph 17’s enumerated requirements and Paragraphs 18–19’s constraints. Accordingly, no further CAP elements can be cited from the provided extracts. [Chapter III – Customer Acceptance Policy, Paragraph 16-19, pp.18-21]
scores.reference_answer: The CAP must ensure that no account is opened in anonymous or fictitious/benami names (Paragraph 17(1)) and that no account is opened where the bank cannot apply appropriate CDD measures due to customer non-cooperation (Paragraph 17(2)). These elements interact by creating a 'stop-go' gate at the point of entry. Furthermore, Paragraph 17(9) requires the bank to verify that the customer's identity does not match any person or entity in the UN Sanctions Lists. This interaction ensures that anonymity is precluded not just at a local name level but also at an international security level.

For each CAP element, state the specific controls or procedures that prevent onboarding of anonymous entities (e.g., ID/verification, BO checks, non-face-to-face safeguards).
Explain how these CAP elements interact with one another (e.g., risk-based segmentation leading to EDD, verification feeding BO checks) to ensure anonymous entities cannot be onboarded.

scores.system_answer:
## 1) What Paragraph 8 mandates and why it is significant (Group-wide AML/CFT programmes)

### A. It is a statutory-aligned obligation for groups
- Paragraph 8 links the requirement directly to the PML Rules and the bank’s obligations under **Chapter IV of the PML Act, 2002**, stating that “**groups shall implement group-wide policies for the purpose of discharging obligations under the provisions of Chapter IV of the PML Act, 2002**.” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]
- It then operationalises this at bank level: “**every bank which is part of a group, shall implement group-wide programmes against money laundering and terror financing** …” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

### B. It makes information-sharing a required design feature of the AML/CFT programme
- Paragraph 8 explicitly requires that the group-wide programmes include “**group-wide policies for sharing information required for the purposes of client due diligence, money laundering, and terrorist finance risk management**.” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]
- This is significant because the Directions also require banks to implement an ML/TF risk framework and controls:
  - Banks must apply a “**Risk Based Approach (RBA)**” and “**implement a CDD programme**” aligned to identified ML/TF risks. [Other General Guidelines, Paragraph 11, pp.18-21]
  - Banks must conduct periodic “**Money Laundering (ML) and Terrorist Financing (TF) Risk Assessment**” exercises to identify and mitigate risk across “clients… products… transactions or delivery channels, etc.” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 10(1), pp.16-17]

### C. It builds safeguards into the programme—confidentiality and anti–tipping-off are part of the mandate
- Paragraph 8 requires that these programmes “**shall include adequate safeguards on the confidentiality and use of information exchanged, including safeguards to prevent tipping-off**.” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

## 2) Why information sharing within a group is a necessity under the 2025 Directions

Information sharing is positioned as a necessity because the Directions require it to enable core AML/CFT duties (CDD and risk management), and they also carve out an explicit allowance for such sharing even where confidentiality constraints exist.

### A. Necessity #1: It is expressly required for CDD and ML/TF risk management
- The Directions mandate group-wide policies specifically for “**sharing information required for the purposes of client due diligence**” and for “**money laundering, and terrorist finance risk management**.” [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

### B. Necessity #2: The Directions contemplate sharing AML analysis within the group even though STR/record-related confidentiality is otherwise required
- The Directions require strict confidentiality around record maintenance and reporting to FIU-IND: “**The bank, its directors, officers, and all employees shall ensure that the fact of maintenance of records … and furnishing of the information to the Director is confidential.**” [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55]
- But they explicitly clarify that this confidentiality “**shall not inhibit sharing of information under paragraph 8 of these Directions of any analysis of transactions and activities which appear unusual, if the bank has done any such analysis.**” [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55]
- This is operationally reinforced by record-management expectations: the definition of records includes “**results of any analysis undertaken**.” [Chapter VII – Record Management, Paragraph 50 (Explanation), pp.52-53]

### C. Necessity #3: The bank’s governance and compliance roles are built around monitoring/reporting/sharing
- The Directions assign the **Principal Officer** responsibility for “**ensuring compliance, monitoring transactions, and sharing and reporting information as required under the law / regulations**.” [Other General Guidelines, Paragraph 15(1), pp.18-21]
- The bank must implement controls and monitoring under the RBA/CDD programme and “**monitor the implementation of the controls and enhance them if necessary.**” [Other General Guidelines, Paragraph 11, pp.18-21]

## 3) Why information sharing within a group is also a risk under the 2025 Directions

The same sharing that is required for effective CDD and group-wide risk management increases the risk of (i) confidentiality breaches, (ii) improper use of exchanged information, and (iii) “tipping-off”—all of which the Directions explicitly treat as concerns that must be guarded against.

### A. Risk #1: Tipping-off risk (explicitly identified in Paragraph 8)
- Paragraph 8 mandates safeguards “**to prevent tipping-off**” as part of the group-wide information-sharing programme—this makes “tipping-off” a primary risk arising from intra-group information exchange. [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

### B. Risk #2: Confidentiality constraints—information sharing must not undermine confidentiality duties
- Even beyond Paragraph 8’s safeguards, the Directions impose confidentiality at multiple points:
  - STR/record-reporting confidentiality: “**The bank, its directors, officers, and all employees shall ensure that the fact of maintenance of records … and furnishing of the information to the Director is confidential.**” [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55]
  - Customer risk-categorisation confidentiality: the bank “**shall keep the risk categorisation of a customer and the specific reasons for such categorisation confidential and shall not reveal this information to the customer to avoid tipping off.**” [Chapter IV – Risk Management, Paragraph 20(4), p.22]
- Therefore, intra-group sharing is “risky” because it must be implemented without violating these confidentiality and anti–tipping-off constraints, which the Directions treat as essential controls. [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55; Chapter IV – Risk Management, Paragraph 20(4), p.22; Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

### C. Risk #3: Risk of misuse of information (explicitly anticipated by Paragraph 8)
- Paragraph 8 requires “**adequate safeguards on the confidentiality and use of information exchanged**,” indicating the compliance risk that shared information could be used inappropriately or disseminated beyond authorised need-to-know channels if safeguards are inadequate. [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]

## 4) Practical compliance framing strictly from the Directions (what the programme must achieve)
- For banks that are part of a group, a compliant group-wide AML/CFT programme must, at minimum:
  - Include **group-wide information-sharing policies** for CDD and ML/TF risk management. [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]
  - Include **confidentiality and permitted-use safeguards**, and **anti–tipping-off safeguards**. [Chapter II – General (Board of Directors / Policies related guidelines), Paragraph 8, pp.16-17]
  - Be designed so that confidentiality duties (e.g., FIU reporting/record confidentiality) are preserved while still enabling the permitted/required intra-group sharing of “analysis of transactions and activities which appear unusual.” [Chapter VIII – Reporting Requirements to Financial Intelligence Unit – India, Paragraph 54, pp.54-55]
scores.reference_answer: Paragraph 8 requires every bank that is part of a corporate group to implement group-wide programs against ML/TF. Information sharing is a necessity because it allows for a holistic view of a customer who may have relationships with different subsidiaries of the same parent company. However, it is a risk because it must include 'adequate safeguards on the confidentiality and use of information exchanged,' including the prevention of 'tipping-off'. The conceptual challenge for banks is to build a unified risk profile without compromising the legal and jurisdictional firewalls that protect customer privacy across different regulated entities.

What mitigation measures or safeguards does the 2025 Directions recommend for intra-group information sharing (e.g., formal agreements, need-to-know, encryption, legal assessments)?

scores.system_answer:
## 1) Definition of “Shell Bank” under the 2025 framework

The 2025 framework defines a **“Shell Bank”** as **“a bank that has no physical presence in the country in which it is incorporated and licensed, and which is unaffiliated with a regulated financial group that is subject to effective consolidated supervision.”** [C. Definitions, pp.12-13, Paragraph (xv)]

The framework further clarifies the meaning of **“physical presence”** as **“meaningful mind and management located within a country”**, and explicitly states that **“[t]he existence simply of a local agent or low-level staff does not constitute physical presence.”** [C. Definitions, pp.12-13, Paragraph (xv)]

## 2) Prohibition on correspondent banking relationships with Shell Banks

Under **Paragraph 73 (Correspondent Banking)**, banks are required to maintain a Board-approved (or Chairman/CEO/MD committee-approved) policy for approving cross-border correspondent banking relationships and apply specific controls in addition to normal CDD. [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.65, Paragraph 73]

Within these mandatory conditions, the framework imposes an explicit prohibition:

- **“The bank shall not enter into or continue a correspondent relationship with a shell bank.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.65, Paragraph 73(7)]

## 3) Why correspondent banking relationships with Shell Banks are treated as strictly prohibited (what the framework shows)

The framework’s **reason for strict prohibition is reflected through the definitional and control architecture it establishes for correspondent banking**, namely:

- Correspondent banking due diligence requires the bank to determine, from publicly available information, **“the reputation of the respondent bank and the quality of supervision”** and to **“assess the respondent bank’s AML / CFT controls.”** [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.65, Paragraph 73(1)]
- The Shell Bank definition identifies a bank that lacks **“physical presence”** (i.e., “meaningful mind and management”) and is **“unaffiliated with a regulated financial group that is subject to effective consolidated supervision.”** [C. Definitions, pp.12-13, Paragraph (xv)]

**What is not explicitly stated in the provided sections:** the text does not separately articulate a narrative “because” clause (e.g., an express statement that shell banks are prohibited due to heightened ML/TF risk). The strictness is established directly by the mandatory wording **“shall not enter into or continue”** in Paragraph 73(7). [Chapter X – Other Instructions, p.65, Paragraph 73(7)]
scores.reference_answer: A shell bank is a bank that has no physical presence (meaningful mind and management) in the country where it is incorporated and is unaffiliated with a regulated financial group (Paragraph 5(2)(xv)). Correspondent relationships with shell banks are prohibited under Paragraph 73(7) because such entities are inherently high-risk. Without a physical presence or consolidated supervision, shell banks can easily be used to layer and integrate illicit funds with zero regulatory oversight. The prohibition extends to ensuring that respondent banks do not allow their own accounts to be used by shell banks, creating a 'clean chain' requirement in international banking.

What provisions or rationale in the 2025 framework explain why correspondent banking relationships with shell banks are strictly prohibited?
